title: Disentangling Spin Pumping and Two-Magnon Scattering Contributions to Gilbert Damping in YIG/V Bilayers
authors: S. Elkady, A. Tlais, H. Reslan, S. Isber, M. Haidar
abstract: In this work, we investigate the magnetic damping and spin pumping response of YIG-based bilayers incorporating vanadium (V) as the normal metal layer via broadband ferromagnetic resonance (FMR) measurements as a function of YIG thickness. We show that the apparent enhancement of the Gilbert damping in YIG/V bilayers cannot be solely attributed to spin pumping. Instead, two-magnon scattering (TMS) plays a dominant role in governing the thickness dependence of the damping in the nanometer regime. By applying a thickness-dependent damping model that accounts for both spin pumping and two-magnon scattering contributions, we successfully disentangle the different relaxation contributions. Our analysis reveals that neglecting two-magnon scattering leads to an overestimation of the spin-pumping contribution and consequently to unphysically large values of the effective spin-mixing conductance. After isolating the intrinsic spin pumping contribution, we extract a thickness-independent effective spin-mixing conductance of $g^{\uparrow\downarrow}_{\mathrm{eff}} = 1.33 \times 10^{18}~\mathrm{m^{-2}}$. These findings provide a more accurate framework for quantifying spin transport parameters in FM/HM systems and emphasize the necessity of accounting for extrinsic damping mechanisms when interpreting spin pumping and inverse spin Hall effect experiments.
summary: This work investigates magnetic damping in YIG/V bilayers using FMR measurements to separate the effects of spin pumping and two-magnon scattering (TMS). The authors demonstrate that TMS is the dominant mechanism controlling the thickness dependence of damping, correcting previous analyses that overestimated the spin-pumping contribution. This provides a more accurate framework for quantifying spin transport parameters in spintronic devices.
main_problem: The study aims to accurately determine the relative contributions of spin pumping and two-magnon scattering (TMS) to the total Gilbert damping observed in YIG/V magnetic bilayers.
main_result: The analysis shows that TMS plays a dominant role in governing the thickness dependence of the damping in the nanometer regime, and neglecting it leads to a significant overestimation of the spin-pumping contribution.
method: The authors used broadband Ferromagnetic Resonance (FMR) measurements combined with a sophisticated, thickness-dependent damping model that explicitly accounts for both spin pumping and TMS contributions.
model_or_system: The physical system is Yttrium iron garnet (YIG) ferromagnetic layers grown on a Vanadium (V) normal metal layer. The analysis models the total damping ($\alpha$) as a sum of contributions from spin pumping ($\alpha_{sp}$) and two-magnon scattering ($\alpha_{TMS}$), alongside the intrinsic damping.
key_observables: Gilbert damping parameter ($\alpha$), FMR linewidth ($\Delta H$), and the effective spin-mixing conductance ($g^{\uparrow\downarrow}_{\mathrm{eff}}$).
important_parameters: The extracted effective spin-mixing conductance is found to be $g^{\uparrow\downarrow}_{\mathrm{eff}} = 1.33 	imes 10^{18}~\mathrm{m^{-2}}$, which is thickness-independent.
main_assumptions: The primary assumption is that the total damping can be accurately decomposed into distinct, quantifiable contributions from spin pumping and TMS using established theoretical models.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate FMR spectra, the thickness dependence of the magnetization, and crucially, the separation of the total damping enhancement into components attributable to spin pumping and TMS.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first measuring the damping enhancement via FMR, then applying a comprehensive model that incorporates both spin pumping and TMS, and finally using this combined model to disentangle the contributions and extract accurate material parameters.
why_interesting: While focused on condensed matter magnetism, the rigorous separation of damping mechanisms (spin pumping vs. extrinsic scattering) is fundamentally related to understanding energy dissipation and spin relaxation in nanoscale magnetic systems, which has parallels in open quantum system dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
